Updating the Standard
Israeli Standards are reviewed periodically at least every five years, in order to adapt them to scientific
and technological developments. Users of Standards should ascertain that they are in possession of the
latest edition of the Standard including its Amendments.
A document appearing in the "Reshumot" (The Israeli Official Journal) as an Amendment may be a
separate Amendment, or an Amendment incorporated into the Standard.
Standard validity
An Israeli Standard including revisions takes effect from its publication date in "Reshumot".

Standard validity
An Israeli Standard including revisions takes effect from its publication date in "Reshumot".
Whether the document or parts of it are Official should be checked. An Official Standard or Amendment
(in whole or in part) takes effect 60 days following publication of the notice in the "Reshumot", unless
the notice states otherwise for the effective date.

SI 6464 (2017)
1
Foreword
The purpose of this Israeli Standard is to allow the use of natural gas within the Israeli industry, by
facilitating the connection of industrial factories to the natural gas supply system, while ensuring the
safety of people, plant and property.

ry, by
facilitating the connection of industrial factories to the natural gas supply system, while ensuring the
safety of people, plant and property.
Requirements related to natural gas infrastructure (transmission system, distribution system and supply
system) are covered by the Israeli Standard SI 5664 (all parts). This Israeli Standard covers requirements
that are relevant to appliances application within the industrial premises.
This Israeli Standard is intended for End-Users, IAA and related systems' manufacturers and Inspection
Bodies, as defined in this Israeli Standard.
The applicability of this Israeli Standard is methodologically divided into four chapters:
(1) Appliance Applications;
(2) Workplace Environment;
(3) Testing & Approval processes;
(4) Fuel Gas Supply Pipework system - design, construction, operation and maintenance.
Accordingly, the four main chapters of this Israeli Standard are:
• Chapter 4 - Industrial Appliances Application (IAA)
Specifying requirements related to every appliance (furnace, boiler, etc.) including all
subsystems, accessories, and supporting systems.
• Chapter 5 - Safety of the workplace environment

ry appliance (furnace, boiler, etc.) including all
subsystems, accessories, and supporting systems.
• Chapter 5 - Safety of the workplace environment
Specifying requirements related to the workplace environment of Industrial Appliances
Application, providing safety and health requirements for employees, to prevent loss of assets,
and protect against any hazardous situations related to natural gas, e.g. ventilation of explosive
atmosphere, venting gas to a safe place, etc.
• Chapter 6 - Testing and Approval Process
Specifying the procedures and stages which need to be obtained (I) approval for installation, (II)
approval for operation, and (III) periodic surveillance and on-site tests.
To achieve an efficient Testing and Approval process, it is recommended to include the
Inspection Body (IB) at the earliest stages of the process as possible.
• Chapter 7 – Fuel Gas Supply Pipework
Specifying requirements related to the pipework within the boundaries of the plant, including
its testing and approval process.

SI 6464 (2017)
2
Introduction to the Israeli Standard
This Israeli Standard is divided into two sections, including two separate paths:
o SECTION 1 - European path
o SECTION 2 - American path.
To demonstrate compliance with this Israeli Standard, its appliers may choose one of the paths (i.e. the
European path, or the American path). Once a certain path is selected, it shall be followed completely.
The European path of this Israeli Standard is based on:
(i) International ISO and IEC standards and codes
(ii) Applicable directives and regulations in Europe;
(iii) Harmonized standards to these EU directives and regulations;
(iv) Non-harmonized standards and other Reference Documents such as technical guidelines;
(v) Rules and procedures of the IECEx System, International Explosion-protection
certification system for equipment, service facilities and personnel, based on IEC
standards (see: www.iecex.com)
The American path of this Israeli Standard is based on:
(i) International ISO and IEC standards and codes
(ii) Applicable standards in USA.

ecex.com)

Foreword to this Israeli Standard).
Some clauses / chapters are identical in both paths, i.e., the European version of the relevant clause /
chapter is also applicable in the American path. In such cases, references are made in the American
path to the parallel clause / chapter in the European path.

SI 6464 (2017)
5
1 Scope
This Israeli Standard specifies safety requirements for new and existing natural gas systems that are
part of industrial natural gas consuming appliances (hereafter Industrial Appliance Application, IAA),
Within these IAA's boundaries, the following are included:
o The Gas Train;
o The burning system and the combustion air (or oxygen) system;
o Burning Management System (BMS);
o Related accessories and auxiliary systems;

esources, e.g. energy performance of the appliances.
Any IAA not included in the scope of the current edition of this Israeli Standard, including all
equipment detailed in the previous paragraph and equipment manufactured as per requirements
specified in relevant American Standards, are covered by the fourth amendment of the Gas Law
(Safety and Licensing).
NOTES to this Israeli Standard:
(1) If the requirements brought in any clause of this Israeli Standard differ from or conflict with the
requirements in any existing standard, the requirements in this Israeli Standard shall apply.
(2) This Israeli Standard, SI 6464, makes normative references to Israeli Standards and foreign
standards. If, during a period of 12 months from the date of publication of this Israeli Standard in
official records, new editions to one of these standards, or more, are published, then both the new
editions of these standards and the old editions of these standards, including corrigendum issued if
any, shall be valid for 12 months for the purpose of using this Israeli Standard.
(3) This Israeli Standard, SI 6464, makes normative references to Israeli Standards adopting foreign

for the purpose of using this Israeli Standard.

standards (European Standards or International Standards).

SI 6464 (2017)
7
With respect to these normatively referenced Israeli standards, the following is applicable:
- If the Israeli Standard is identical to the adopted foreign standard (no national deviations), the
certificate of compliance with the foreign standard can be accepted by the Inspection Body (see
3.37).
- If the Israeli Standard adopts the foreign standard, incorporating national deviations, the
Inspection Body can decide whether to accept the certificate of compliance with the foreign
standard, or to require another certificate according to the normatively referenced Israeli
Standard.

SI 6464 (2017)
12
3 Terms & Definitions
3.1 Industrial Appliance Application (IAA)
industrial natural gas consuming process installation using combustion air (or equivalent) and natural
gas to feed any industrial process
The boundaries of the IAA are from the manual isolating valve (located nearest downstream of the
first control valve), and up to the release point of the flue gas into the atmosphere, through any given
exhaust system.
NOTE - any premixes of NG with other substances (e.g. steam) prepared for processes, such as
chemical reforming, is not included in the definition of IAA.
3.1.1 Complete IAA (CIAA)
new installed natural gas system, originally designed to operate on natural gas (purchased and supplied
as a whole unit), which is a part of an IAA
3.1.2 Partial IAA (PIAA)
converted fuel (e.g., fuel oil, gas oil, LPG) to natural gas system, which is a part of an IAA, including
the addition of natural gas equipment and their related support systems for the conversion of an
existing IAA to natural gas operation.
3.2 System

ng
the addition of natural gas equipment and their related support systems for the conversion of an
existing IAA to natural gas operation.
3.2 System
subject of an IAA, generally includes a process, a product, an activity, a facility or a logical system
3.3 Industrial Thermoprocessing Equipment (IThE)
see definition in SI 74600-2
3.4 Pressure Equipment

3.19 Risk Assessment
determination of quantitative or qualitative estimate of risk related to a concrete situation and
a recognized threat (also called hazard).

magnitude of the potential loss (L), and the probability (P) that loss occurs
- Qualitative risk assessment: based on the experiences and statistical values, a risk assessment

triggered by different events such as disoperation caused by human error, hardware failures and

4 Industrial Appliances Application (IAA)
4.1 General Application Rules
This section covers the requirements for technical installations of both new and existing IAAs which
are to be connected to the NG (Natural Gas) grid. IAAs of new plants shall be considered Complete
IAAs (CIAAs), whereas IAAs of existing plants shall be considered as Partial IAAs (PIAAs).
Hereby, “CIAA” or “PIAA” equipment are considered the same. Hence, unsafe “existing equipment”
shall be changed or refitted to a safe level.
The general requirements defined in 4.2 of this Israeli Standard are mandatory requirements for both
new and existing installations. Presumption of conformity shall be achieved when the Specific

is Israeli Standard are mandatory requirements for both
new and existing installations. Presumption of conformity shall be achieved when the Specific
Requirements of 4.3 and 4.4 are fulfilled.
Presumption of conformity shall be achieved when all IAAs meet the General Requirements set in 4.2.
In addition to this, the specific requirements
• set out in 4.3 for new plants with CIAAs, including the interface from isolating valve to the
exhaust gas connection
• set out in 4.4 for existing plants with PIAAs, including the interface from isolating valve to
burner flange
shall be fulfilled.
4.2 General Requirements for IAA
IAAs and all related machinery within the boundaries as defined in the scope of this Israeli Standard
shall be designed and built as to allow safe operation and to present no danger to persons and
properties.
Unburned gas release
Industrial Appliances Application shall be constructed so that gas leakage rate is not dangerous.
Industrial Appliances Application shall be constructed so that gas release during ignition and re-
ignition and after flame extinction is limited in order to avoid a dangerous accumulation of unburned
gases in the appliance.
Ignition

nition and re-
ignition and after flame extinction is limited in order to avoid a dangerous accumulation of unburned
gases in the appliance.
Ignition
Industrial Appliances Application shall be so constructed that, when used normally:
• ignition and re-ignition is smooth,
• cross-lighting is assured.

Combustion
Industrial Appliances Application shall be so constructed that, when used normally, flame stability is
assured and combustion products do not contain unacceptable concentrations of substances harmful to
health.
Industrial Appliances Application shall be so constructed that, when used normally, there is no
accidental release of combustion products.
IAAs connected to a flue gas system for the dispersal of combustion products shall be so constructed
that in abnormal draught conditions there is no release of combustion products in a dangerous quantity
into the concerned room.
Temperatures
Parts of IAA which are intended to be placed in close proximity to the floor or other surfaces shall not
reach temperatures which present danger in the surrounding area.

re intended to be placed in close proximity to the floor or other surfaces shall not
reach temperatures which present danger in the surrounding area.
The surface temperature of knobs and levers of appliances intended to be manipulated shall not present
danger to the user.
Surface temperatures of external parts of IAA intended for industrial use, with the exception of
surfaces or parts which are associated with the transmission of heat, shall not, under operating
conditions, present danger to the user/operator, i.e., the requirements for the appropriate exposure time
as defined in the relevant standards, shall be followed.
Design and construction
• Industrial Appliances Application shall be so constructed that, when used normally, no
instability, distortion, breakage or wear likely to impair their safety can occur.
• Condensation produced at the start-up and/or during use shall not affect the safety of Industrial
Appliances Application.
• Industrial Appliances Application shall be so designed and constructed as to minimize the risk
of explosion in the event of a fire of external origin.
• Industrial Appliances Application shall be so constructed that water and inappropriate air

of explosion in the event of a fire of external origin.
• Industrial Appliances Application shall be so constructed that water and inappropriate air
penetration into the fuel circuit does not occur.
• In the event of a normal fluctuation of auxiliary energy, Industrial Appliances Application
shall continue to operate safely.
• Abnormal fluctuation or failure of auxiliary energy or its restoration shall not lead to an unsafe
situation. (Functional Safety).
• IAA shall be so designed and constructed as to obviate hazards of electrical origin. In
applicable areas, compliance with safety objectives in respect of electrical hazards is laid
down in 4.3.11.

4.2.1 Risk assessment of changes related to the use of natural gas in IAAs
A risk assessment for IAA is generally carried out to determine and minimize the health and safety
hazard levels which occur through IAA.

LOPA and others).

project. Trained and experienced in leading HAZOP studies. Responsible for
communications between project management and the HAZOP team. Plans the study.

4.2.1.1 Functional Safety devices (SIL Loops)
Any claim of functional safety for a component, subsystem or system shall be evaluated against the
recognized functional safety standards IEC 61508 – the relevant parts. A certified product e.g.
transmitter, valve etc., can then be claimed to be Functionally Safe to a particular Safety Integrity
Level (SIL) in a specific range of applications.
• The End-User shall receive a certificate from the device's manufacturer demonstrating
compliance with the requirements of IEC 61508 – the relevant parts, with a test report
describing the scope and limits of performance.
• In case there is no certificate available and a self-declaration has been issued, compliance of
the device with IEC 61508 – the relevant parts shall be confirmed by a Technical Expert
which is accredited according to the applicable desired standard issued by an ISO EN 17025
appointed laboratory.

4.2.1.2 Devices for Explosive Atmospheres
Risk Assessments performed according to the requirements of this Israeli standard are only related to
the application of natural gas.

sive Atmospheres
Risk Assessments performed according to the requirements of this Israeli standard are only related to
the application of natural gas. However, if the IAA is installed in an environment where other sources
of explosive atmosphere exist (e.g., flour dust, fuel vapor), then the devices, which are part of the
natural gas system, shall be compatible for use in this explosive environment.
Devices for use in potentially explosive atmospheres shall be divided into the following three
categories:
- Category I – applicable for coal mining, not included within this Israeli Standard;
- Category II – explosive gases and vapor;
- Category III – environment with dust.
4.2.2 Design and Construction of Natural Gas Pipework and Pressure Equipment within the
Boundary of the IAA
Hazards caused by NG pipework and pressure equipment within the boundary of the IAA, and the
corresponding accessories, shall be minimized.
The following requirements for the design and manufacturing of NG supply equipment with a
maximum allowable pressure greater than 0.5 barg. shall be applied.
The NG equipment shall be designed, manufactured and checked, and if applicable equipped and

owable pressure greater than 0.5 barg. shall be applied.
The NG equipment shall be designed, manufactured and checked, and if applicable equipped and
installed, in such a way as to ensure its safety when put into service in accordance with the
manufacturer’s instructions, or in reasonably foreseeable conditions.
In choosing the most appropriate solutions, the End-User shall ensure that the following principles are
applied by the manufacturer, in the following order:
• Eliminate or reduce hazards as far as is reasonably practical;
• Apply appropriate protection measures against hazards which cannot be eliminated;
• Where appropriate, inform users of residual hazards and indicate whether it is
necessary to take appropriate special measures to reduce the risks at the time of
installation and/or use.
The piping and the corresponding accessories shall be designed for loadings appropriate to its intended
use and other reasonably foreseeable operating conditions. In particular, the following factors shall be
taken into account:
• Internal/external pressure,
• Ambient and operational temperatures,
• Static pressure and mass of contents in operating and test conditions,

count:
• Internal/external pressure,
• Ambient and operational temperatures,
• Static pressure and mass of contents in operating and test conditions,
• Traffic, wind, earthquake loading,
• Reaction forces and moments which result from the supports, attachments, piping,
etc.,
• Corrosion and erosion, fatigue, etc.
Various loadings which can occur at the same time shall be considered, taking into account the
probability of their simultaneous occurrence.

This chapter applies to Complete IAAs which include an IThE/Steam boiler or any other industrial
fuel-consuming appliances as described in the scope of this standard and is constructed and built as
completely new.

Assessment shall be conducted.

The risk assessment team may choose one of the following standards for risk assessment performance:
• ISO 12100 shall be applied for Complete IAA, e.g. for a dedicated burner, pump, etc. This risk
assessment is an iterative process with different possibilities for reducing the risk to an
acceptable tolerance level.
• IEC 61882 shall be applied for a set/combination (system) of different devices/machinery, e.g.

reducing the risk to an

NOTE
HAZOP is often used as a technique for identifying potential hazards in a system and
identifying operability problems likely to lead to non-conforming products or system
application. HAZOP is based on a theory that assumes risk events are caused by deviations
from design or operating intentions. Identification of such deviations is facilitated by using
sets of “guide words” as a systematic list of deviation perspectives.
• Other methods for performing RA are available and specified in all parts of IEC 61511 (e.g.

4.3.3 Combustion Air Passages
The construction of the combustion air pipework shall be so designed and built as to fulfil correctly
their intended purpose. The air supply to any combustion device within the boundaries of the CIAA
shall be designed unrestrictedly. If shut-off valves are installed, these devices shall be safely
controlled. The location of the combustion air intake shall be such as to prevent entry of exhaust.
The ventilation of the building and thereby the Complete IAA shall be such as to allow an adequate
supply of process /combustion air to reach the combustion units under all circumstances and
conditions.
All manual air control devices (registers, valves, etc.) shall be set in their pre-determined positions and
protected against unintentional movement.
The provided combustion air shall reach the burner(s) under all conditions and ducts shall be so
designed as to prevent the back-flow of furnace atmosphere.
Air flow shall comply with the following:
• Monitored by pressure detectors or by flow detectors.

as to prevent the back-flow of furnace atmosphere.
Air flow shall comply with the following:
• Monitored by pressure detectors or by flow detectors.
• Air pressure detectors shall comply with EN 1854.
• Each combustion device shall be provided with an adjustment device for the air flow.
As far as the combustion air passages are covered within SI 74600-2, SI 67600, SI 12953-7 and
SI 12952-8, as relevant, and if it complies with the requirements specified in those standards, no
further requirements are needed.

4.3.4 Gas/Air Ratio of CIAAs
Any chosen gas/air ratio within the boundaries of the CIAA shall be controlled within admissible
limits. The predetermined values of the gas/air ratio within these limits shall be stated in the operating
manual with meaningful protocols. The gas/air ratio monitoring circuit shall be independent of the
control circuit.
Pneumatic gas/air ratio controls shall comply with EN 88-1.
Electronic gas/air ratio controls shall comply with EN 12067-2 if applicable.
Mechanical gas/air ratio controls may be used. The mechanical gas/air ratio may be unmonitored if the

ols shall comply with EN 12067-2 if applicable.
Mechanical gas/air ratio controls may be used. The mechanical gas/air ratio may be unmonitored if the
limits are fixed during the initial adjustment and cannot be removed afterwards.
If the requirements of these standards cannot be complied with (e.g., EN 88-1 or EN 12067-2 cannot
be applied for CHPs), other equivalent safety levels may be applied.
The design of the natural gas/air ratio control shall consider process conditions as well as fuel and
combustion air properties.
The air mass flow rate shall always be in a ratio with the fuel mass flow in order to ensure safe
ignition and that throughout the operating range, a stable and safe combustion is maintained at each
individual burner. The ratio may not be the same value at all operational conditions.
As far as the gas/air ratio are covered within SI 74600-2, SI 67600, as relevant, and if it complies with
the requirements specified in those standards, no further requirements are needed.

4.3.8 Stacks Fed by Several Firings
4.3.8.1 General
The stack shall be designed according to the applicable standards, e.g. SI 67600, SI 74600-2, SI
12952-8, SI 12953-7, in order to ensure proper exhausting of flue gases and safe operation of the
burning system.
4.3.8.2 Common Stack
If a common stack exists, the prescribed risk assessment as laid down in 4.3.1 shall include the
possible hazards related to a common stack.
In general flue, gas flows from several firings entering a common duct shall be so constructed that
• during all operating conditions the stack pressure variates in an acceptable tolerance
• explosive flue gas mixtures are excluded
• unacceptable backpressure to the single firings are prevented
• the temperature of the (common) flue gas is low enough to avoid self-ignition
• external ignition sources are prevented
• in cases where the flue gas ducts of each single burner are controlled with mechanical
dampers, the design and control of each single Burner Management System shall consider all
operational circumstances and implement controlled interlocks, if needed.

sign and control of each single Burner Management System shall consider all
operational circumstances and implement controlled interlocks, if needed.
4.3.9 Pre-Purging of the Combustion Chamber and the Flue Gas Passages
In cases where IAAs covered within SI 74600-2 are concerned, the requirements specified in SI
74600-2 shall be followed. In all the other cases, the requirements to be complied with are as follows:
The combustion chamber and the connected flue gas passes shall be of such a design as to ensure
effective purging. Prior to any firing system start-up, the combustion chamber and the flue gases
passes shall be effectively purged. The operating instructions of the combustion unit shall be followed.
If operation instructions are not defined, the forced ventilation mass flow shall include the combustion
chamber, all flue gas passages up to entering the stack (total volume) so that the total volume is
changed 5 times prior to any firing system start-up. Purging time shall be at least 30 sec.
After any false-start, cutout during normal or abnormal situation, the above procedure shall be
repeated before any re-firing start-up.

least 30 sec.
After any false-start, cutout during normal or abnormal situation, the above procedure shall be
repeated before any re-firing start-up.
Manually operated firings shall also consist of an adequate/effective purging. This shall be ensured
with adequate timing delay.
In some special cases of IThE, the purging conditions can variate from the aforesaid. In such cases,
SI 74600-2 shall be considered.

4.5 Management of Change Procedure
A management of change procedure shall be prepared. The document shall address the impact of newly
installed IAAs, the conversion of IAAs, and/or changes to existing IAAs, components, operating and
maintenance procedures and anything else that could impact gas piping or equipment safety.
This procedure shall specify, among others, the instructions to guide the user with respect to the activities
needed during the performance of a change in the plant, including:
• Mapping all the means (expected and unexpected) that are due to be affected by the change.
• Assessment of all the impacts relevant to performing the change.
• Defining "stopping points" to examine and to define whether the change is needed and how to
promote it (including the relevant complementary steps), before performing it.

5 Workplace Environment
5.0 General
This chapter addresses possible Workplace Environment hazards that may be associated with the
installation of new natural gas fired equipment, natural gas conversions, and their associated fuel
piping systems. The concepts for protecting personnel and property in this chapter include the
evaluation of flammable gas-air mixture risks from natural gas leakage or intentional releases from
venting or purging operations within or near an installation.
5.1 Ventilation of Workplace Environment
The Technical Expert shall perform analysis and testing of the level of ventilation in the
surrounding of the appliance and shall assure that ventilation conditions comply with the
following requirements:
- Installed equipment shall be suitable to the conditions to which the appliance or its
components are exposed to (max.

th the
following requirements:
- Installed equipment shall be suitable to the conditions to which the appliance or its
components are exposed to (max. temperature and humidity), and in any case, it shall
withstand temperatures in the range of -20C to +60C.
- Ventilation level shall be such that allows the proper operation of the burner blowers,
chimneys, etc. and shall not interfere with combustion air requirements (for additional
information, see BS 6644 or other applicable standards).
- Ventilation level shall be such that assures proper work conditions (max. temperature and
humidity) for the operators, as per the requirements of the Work safety ordinance.
- Ventilation air shall be provided as to remove any combustion by-products and process
fumes that could pose a safety risk to workers (e.g. CO, flue gas).
- Explosive atmospheres shall be ventilated or mitigated, according to 5.2.
5.2 Explosive Atmospheres of the Workplace Environment

Flammable gases can be released from leaks, designed vent locations, and component vents.

component selection, and vent discharge point locations.
If the risk from natural gas leakage is sufficiently small, then area classification shall not be
required where an installation complies with all the following:

5.2.2 Classification of Ex-Zones
Classification of Ex-Zones, (zones where flammable mixtures from leaks or discharges can
occur), shall be performed in accordance with the requirements of NFPA 497 or SI 60079-10.1
or other equivalent Israeli Standards, (i.e., SI 25000, SI 16000).
Once zones (groupings of piping, vent discharges, etc.) are evaluated, measures to prevent or
minimize risks related to these shall be undertaken.
Equipment enclosures that contain natural gas fuel trains, instruments, and piping can be classified
as hazardous or non-hazardous depending on equipment and conditions inside. Leaks inside these
cabinets can occur and can make for flammable atmospheres which can be hazardous.
5.2.3 Ventilation of Ex-Zones
The relative hazard of Ex-Zones can be influenced by the ventilation of the area where a

pheres which can be hazardous.
5.2.3 Ventilation of Ex-Zones
The relative hazard of Ex-Zones can be influenced by the ventilation of the area where a
dangerous explosive atmosphere can occur (amount of dilution air flow and whether this is by
natural versus mechanical ventilation).
During natural ventilation, the openings in the building, their location, the air temperatures (inside
versus outside) and the wind direction and speed have an influence of the efficiency of the
ventilation.
The calculation of the wall openings by natural ventilation is described in SI 60079-10-1.
It shall be assured that adequate ventilation is provided, as to maintain any plant areas with NG
leaks below 20% LFL (Lower Flammable Limit) and that the electrical equipment, located in
classified explosive zones, is appropriate for the level of hazard. If those conditions cannot be
met, the Technical Expert shall perform a hazard analysis, to find other methods for mitigation of
the risk.

5.2.5 Selection of Equipment
To select the appropriate electrical equipment for hazardous zones, the following is required:
• Electrical equipment shall be selected according to the relevant European or International
Standards, or should be selected according to the American Standard NFPA 70.
• Classification of the hazardous area including the equipment protection level requirements
where applicable.
• Where applicable, gas or vapor classification in relation to the group or subgroup of the
electrical equipment.
• Intended application of the equipment.
Equipment protection level (EPL) requirements should be recorded on an area classification
drawing.

6 Testing and Approval
6.1 General
This chapter outlines the process of Testing and Approval of any PIAA and CIAA using natural gas
burning appliance.
The End-User shall separately submit to the IB, for each IAA, an individual application for approval via
an "Approval Application Form" (see 6.4.4.3) before installing and operating these IAAs (Serviceability
approval).
The specific requirements for the different stages of Testing and Approval (Stage I: Approval for
installation, and Stage II: Approval for operation - Serviceability Approval) are illustrated in Figure 6-1
and Figure 6-2.
NOTE:The goal of this chapter is to make sure that the End-User understands the regulatory and
technical requirements and processes to approve natural gas appliance installations (IAA) in industrial
premises for safe use.

with a particular expertise and professional qualification.

4.2.4 "NOT PASS" Category - Gap Report (by the Technical Expert)
6.4.2.4.1 Demonstration of Compliance to Latest Editions of the Applicable Standards
A gap report is not required for items determined as "PASS" category.
Items determined as "NOT PASS" category shall be resolved through a gap report.
A Technical Expert(s) (appointed by the End-User and approved by the IB) shall review the
EOC and develop a Gap Report (based on the EOC) for each item or system, specifying issues
being incorrect, incomplete, or not conforming to the relevant standard (the edition valid at the
date of submitting the "Approval Application Form").
6.4.2.4.1.1 Gap Resolution
Each item of non-compliance, addressed in the Gap Report, shall be accompanied by a method
of bringing the non-compliant item into compliance or a recommendation of why the non-
compliant item is not relevant to the safety of the installation.
The IB shall review the Gap Report and accept or reject the recommendations, including
justifications (based on the relevant documents or other parallel standards).
The Technical Expert shall review the rejected items and submit a revised Gap Report

s (based on the relevant documents or other parallel standards).
The Technical Expert shall review the rejected items and submit a revised Gap Report
identifying methods to bring remaining non-compliant items into compliance.
6.4.2.4.2 Demonstration of Compliance to Earlier Editions of the Applicable Standards
If an IAA complies with the requirements of earlier editions of the applicable standards, the
manufacturer shall provide a demonstration of compliance with the former edition of the
applicable standard and deliver a gap report specifying the gaps between the new and former
editions of these applicable standards through a self-declaration.
If the manufacturer is not available, the Technical Expert shall provide an approved gap
analysis.
For gap resolution, 6.4.2.4.1.1 shall be followed.
Once the gaps are resolved, a second demonstration of compliance to the current edition of the
applicable standard shall be delivered through a self-declaration.
6.4.2.5 Stage I - Approval for Installation
Approval for Installation shall be granted only for new IAAs and for the converted parts of
existing IAAs.

.4.2.5 Stage I - Approval for Installation
Approval for Installation shall be granted only for new IAAs and for the converted parts of
existing IAAs.
If all gaps (according to the gap report issued by the Technical Expert) have been successfully
fulfilled, the IB shall issue an Approval for Installation to the End-User.

methods are given in Chapter 4 in the relevant subchapter (Gas burner, IThE, Vessel
Boiler etc.).
• assessment of the gas connection and its possible leakages.
The measurement methods and instruments are given in 4.3.2.
• general assessment of the appliance installation against the workplace environment
safety requirements.
All on-site Testing and Approvals / validations made by the IB are visual Testing and
Approvals and are limited to witnessing of the performed tests (e.g., SAT – Site Acceptance
Tests) and the tests themselves shall be performed solely by the End-User or its appointed
Technical Expert.
The test equipment shall be provided by the responsible End-User or its appointed Technical
Expert. The IB shall assure that the test equipment is suitable and calibrated as per regulation
and/or manufacturers guideline. However, the IB may decide at any time to make use of its
own measurement equipment.

After a successful performance of the installation (piping), functional tests and cold
commissioning (without Natural Gas), the IB shall review the test reports and, if positive, shall
issue, alongside the test report, a written approval for "Gassing-Up".
NOTE: Additional guidance for commissioning is given in IGEM/UP/4.
6.4.2.6.2 Permission for "Gassing-up" and Hot Commissioning
Once permission for "Gassing-Up" has been issued by the IB, the End-User or its appointed
Technical Expert shall complete Cold Commissioning (with Natural Gas), supervised by the
IB (The IB shall review the relevant test report issues by the End-User).
If the necessary tests of the cold commissioning are assessed by the IB as being finally positive
and acceptable, the IB shall issue a Permission for Hot Commissioning which includes the test
protocol from the previous step.

6.4.2.6.3 Serviceability Approval

a test report), and complete all items remained unresolved (originated in the EOC report).
The IB shall review the HC test report and assure completion of the remaining items and if

Approval).

Law (Safety and Licensing) and shall be undertaken with periodic surveillance, maintenance
and operations tests with IB approved results.

6.4.3 Maintenance and Operation Tests (by the End-User)
Once Approval for Operation (Serviceability Approval) has been issued, maintenance and
operation, periodic surveillance, including performing any change in the IAA (see 4.5) shall be
undertaken by the End-User and shall be its full responsibility.
Maintenance, and the intervals of the maintenance tests, may depend on the possible hazards
occurred during risk assessment of the appliance installation and the Workplace Environment,
based on the engineering documents, on the requirements of certain standards, and on the
manufacturer’s instructions, and shall be approved by the IB.
The requirements of 6.4.3 shall be performed according to the relevant standards quoted in this

urer’s instructions, and shall be approved by the IB.
The requirements of 6.4.3 shall be performed according to the relevant standards quoted in this
Israeli Standard, manuals provided by the equipment manufacturer(s), which were approved by the
IB, when the Approval for Operation (Serviceability Approval) was issued.
The test plan for the periodic maintenance shall contain, at least, the following tests (these shall be
well documented and signed by the party performing the tests and by the responsible party):
• Assessment of the natural gas connection and its possible leakages. Measurement methods
and instruments are given in 4.3.2.
• A general assessment of the appliance installation against the Workplace Environment safety
requirements.
• A general test of any leakages.
• A general test of any physical damage to any component which is a part of the IAA.
• A general test of the operation of all sub-systems including the burning systems and its
controls, as per the original design and manufacturer's instructions.
NOTE: If there are no manufacturer's instructions, the Technical Expert shall create detailed
instructions for the periodic maintenance and inspections.

E: If there are no manufacturer's instructions, the Technical Expert shall create detailed
instructions for the periodic maintenance and inspections.
• A test of any SIS (Safety Instrumented System) loop.
6.4.4 Renewal of the Serviceability Approval
Serviceability Approval granted to the End-User shall be renewed, within intervals as required
by The Gas Law (Safety and Licensing).
For this renewal of the Serviceability Approval, the IB shall issue the new Serviceability
Approval, if the following requirements (6.4.4.1) are complied with:
6.4.4.1 Maintenance Review by the IB
At renewal of the Serviceability Approval according to the requirements of The Gas Law
(Safety and Licensing), the IB shall check the maintenance undertaken by the End-User, as per
approved maintenance plan.

7.1.2 Before the End-User can install and subsequently operate an Industrial Gas Installation system, the
End-User shall apply for an approval and such approval shall be granted by the IB prior to
commencement of any installation related activity.
7.1.3 Exemptions may be granted by the IB only upon written request by the End-User subject to agreed
restrictions and conditions. Exemptions shall be considered on a case-by-case basis and a given
exemption can be withdrawn if:
7.1.3.1 The data furnished by the End-User to obtain it appear to be incorrect or incomplete to
the extent that the exemption would not have been granted if the actual data had been
known at the time the decision was made.
7.1.3.2 The circumstances on the basis of which it was granted have changed to the extent that
the exemption would not have been granted if the altered circumstances had existed at
the time the decision was made.
7.1.3.3 It cannot be maintained in case a safety hazard develops out of it.

Any Start-up and/or Hot Commissioning operations of the System shall only commence after the End-

7.6 Operations and Emergency
7.6.1 Procedure Details
The End-User shall prepare a copy of an emergency and operations procedure to be reviewed by the IB.
This procedure shall be written in Hebrew at least, and in addition, in languages as per requested by the
IB.
A situation shall be considered "emergency" if it poses an immediate threat to life, health, property or
the environment.
7.6.1.1 The emergency procedure shall take into account the natural gas facilities in the End-User
premises as well as any potential mutual influences possible from other, nearby gas works and/or
other infrastructure in the area. This plan shall provide all possible emergencies related to the
operation of an IAA.
7.6.1.2 The End-User shall identify and classify emergencies.

a. This plan shall provide all possible emergencies related to the
operation of an IAA.
7.6.1.2 The End-User shall identify and classify emergencies.
7.6.1.3 For all cases the End-User shall analyze the short term effects and the long term effects of such
emergency and the response to the occurrence.
7.6.1.4 For each type of emergency, the End-User shall have a pre-established response and effect
mitigation plan with the objective of avoiding or, at least, limiting the negative effect. To
accomplish this, the End-User shall demonstrate to the IB that an effect mitigation / limitation
philosophy is in place, that a personnel rescue and evacuation procedures is in place, that
associated spare/repair materials are in stock, that specialist contracts are in place, that repair
and clean-up procedures are developed, etc.
7.6.1.5 This emergency procedure shall also include a valve by valve opening and closing status to
assure proper closure in the event of an emergency.

for the design life of the complete System. The inspection and maintenance programme shall
confirm the pipe system integrity. The pipeline integrity could be jeopardized as the result of

SI 6464 (2017)
103
Annex 7.0 - Factories Operations, Maintenance and Emergency Guidance Annex -
Procedures, Documents and Guidelines for the Safe Operation and Maintenance of a
Natural Gas Supply System within Factory Premises
(Informative)
7.0 (a) INTRODUCTION
Each plant shall have procedures for operation, maintenance and emergency (OM&E). For this
purpose, one may use the procedures described in this annex, as applicable.
The purpose of this document is to provide a high level synopsis of the compliance requirements
documents, necessary to ensure the safe and successful Operations and Maintenance of the natural
gas piped supply systems to end consumers/gas appliances.
The Factories Operation, Maintenance & Emergency Guidance Document (FOMEGD hereinafter)
document has been written to cover both existing & future planned Operations & Maintenance
requirements. Regular review & updating of the compliance documentation outlined herein shall be
required and be maintained in order to ensure that the documentation remains current.
The document hierarchy process and each individual document are developed and managed using a

order to ensure that the documentation remains current.
The document hierarchy process and each individual document are developed and managed using a
management control framework, often referred to as POPIMAR, which is represented as a continuous

7.0 (b) SCOPE
This guidance document covers the natural gas systems within factory premises; downstream of the
outlet isolating valve on the transmission/distribution skid and up to the isolating valves in the natural
gas appliances/end consumers’ trains (excluding the natural gas appliances/end consumers). The
scope of this document aligns with the clause 7.
Additionally, all maintenance facilities, plant and equipment, vehicles and tools considered necessary
to undertake the piped system OM&E activities are covered. The factory general manager and gas
safety officer are to provide an annual approval of compliance with this certificate to as per appendix
2 of the IGID.
7.0 (c) REGULATORY AND OPERATIONAL BUSINESS DRIVERS
The onus is on the End User to ensure and verify that the piped gas supply system assets within the

0 (c) REGULATORY AND OPERATIONAL BUSINESS DRIVERS
The onus is on the End User to ensure and verify that the piped gas supply system assets within the
factory are operated efficiently and effectively, to the highest possible safety standards. This is
achieved by designing, constructing, operating and maintaining these assets in compliance with the
requirements of International and National codes & standards, manufacturer’s and supplier’s
minimum recommendations, and by application of current ‘best practice’.
The End User shall provide a comprehensive report detailing the maintenance activities performed on
the gas system assets. The frequency to providing this report shall be according to his discretion, and
not more than once a year.
7.0 (c-1) COPORATE VALUES AND PRIORITIES
The End User shall develop a set of corporate goals to achieve excellence throughout all business
activities & operations. This means that, as far as possible, no harm will result to employees, the
public at large or the environment from the company’s activities.

7.0 (e) SYNOPSIS OF LEVEL 2 DOCUMENTS
7.0 (e-1) OPERATIONS PERMIT TO WORK SYSTEM
The purpose of the Permit to Work System is to ensure that a safe working environment is achieved
by providing management control over the various activities which may have hazardous interactions.

ensure that a safe working environment is achieved
by providing management control over the various activities which may have hazardous interactions.
The system provides a formal and controlled process that identifies and communicates risks and a
hazard associated with planned activity, and ensures that appropriate precautions and measures are
implemented so that the activity can proceed and be completed safely. It is important to note that a
Permit to Work is not a permission to carry out a hazardous activity, but is an essential part of a
procedure that provides instruction on how to carry out a hazardous activity safely in a managed and
controlled way.
The Work Permit serves the following purposes:
• To ensure the proper authorization of work.
• To make clear to the person carrying out the activity the risks involved and the precautions to be
taken.
• To ensure that only competent individuals are assigned to manage, authorize and carry out the
work.
• To provide a record showing that the method of work and precautions needed have been checked
by the appropriate authority.
• A properly designed and administered Work Permit System shall eliminate/minimize human errors

eeded have been checked
by the appropriate authority.
• A properly designed and administered Work Permit System shall eliminate/minimize human errors
and hazards. This system shall provide proper and managed control measures in order to protect
the activity performer and others involved, the environment and company property.
• To provide an effective means for safe control of work, to ensure the safety of personnel and the
public, to protect plant and equipment from damage and prevent loss of gas supplies.
• To clearly define the particular part of the network or facilities to be worked on.
• To specify the work to be done.
• To place a time limit on the work to provide a means to avoid conflict between the work and other
activities.
• To ensure that the responsible person supervising the work is fully aware of the hazards and
safety measures that shall be maintained whilst undertaking the work.

7.0 (e-4) HAZARD ASSESSMENT DOCUMENT
The End User shall develop a Factory Hazard Assessment Document for the natural gas
system and assets which identifies the credible hazards which may cause risk to the future
integrity of the system and considers the hazard effects in terms of individual risk and risk to
society in general.
The hazards identified include:

• Impact Damage (Mechanical Interference)
• Fatigue
• Corrosion
• Operator Error
Likely failure modes & consequences of pipeline and AGI's failures are explained within the
document.

7.0 (f-1.1) Inspection & Maintenance Requirements
7.0 (f-1.1.1) Inspection & Maintenance Philosophy
Preventive maintenance is performed as a pro-active measure in the operation of natural gas piping
system assets. Its primary purpose is to prevent equipment failures that adversely affect the reliability
of the piping system and assets.
The Inspection and Maintenance Philosophy defines the desired level of equipment, facility and
piping system reliability. These reliability thresholds are used to establish the nature and the initial
frequency of preventive maintenance routines. The philosophy also outlines a methodology for
adjusting the frequency of these routines based on the results of ongoing statistical trends and
analysis.
The level of corrective maintenance required in a piping system is inversely proportional to the
amount of preventive maintenance that is performed. Some amount of corrective maintenance will be

in a piping system is inversely proportional to the
amount of preventive maintenance that is performed. Some amount of corrective maintenance will be
required no matter how extensive the preventive maintenance program is.

7.0 (f-4) MODIFICATIONS & REPAIR PROCEDURE
Any alterations to any Factory NG piping supply systems and assets shall be subject to a Modification
and Repair Procedure supported by a Facility Change Procedure.
This document ensures that, however minor modification or repair appears to be, it has been
designed appropriately for the system or asset component it is being applied to. The procedure
further requires any modification or repair to be independently appraised by an expert in the particular
field before the work goes ahead. The use of the Modification & Repair Procedure is also appropriate
for record purposes and to ensure the traceability of components.
Design changes shall be submitted for review by an inspector prior to any construction activities.

To ensure a high level of safety and reliability in both the operation and the maintenance of asset

7.0 (f-5) FAULT DATA MANAGEMENT PROCEDURE
To ensure a high level of safety and reliability in both the operation and the maintenance of asset
integrity/condition, it is essential that a system of fault data collection and analysis exists for the
assets associated with the Factory NG supply systems and assets.
This procedure shall detail and harmonize methods of fault data collection and analysis required to
support the continuation of an efficient and cost effective maintenance policy.
It is recommended than an electronic database is developed to hold all fault data in order to readily
analyse and determine failure trends.
7.0 (f-6) DAMAGE, MEASUREMENT & ASSESSMENT
The procedure shall detail the mandatory requirements for measurement and assessment of damage
to operational NG piping.
The requirements are not intended for application to damage found during construction of any new
NG piping prior to commissioning.
In general, piping damage will be one of the following types:
• General and/or local loss of wall thickness by corrosion.
• Loss of wall thickness, gouging, denting, cracking, spalling and combinations thereof, caused by
external mechanical interference.

ss by corrosion.
• Loss of wall thickness, gouging, denting, cracking, spalling and combinations thereof, caused by
external mechanical interference.
• Buckling, denting, cracking and combinations thereof, caused by ground movement.
• Cracking caused by stress corrosion or fatigue.
• Breaks and/or disbandment of protective coating only.
Personnel required toundertake either damage measurement or damage assessment shall be
competent to undertake their specific duties via applicable training, certification and experience.

8.1.3 Case example
A HAZOP study is a detailed hazard and operability problem identification process, carried out
by a team. The examination relies on specialists from various disciplines with appropriate skills and
experience who display intuition and good judgement. The performing of the HAZOP has to be
documented, examples can be found in the standard.
Figure 8-3 shows a possible outcome of a HAZOP. The unsafe state overpressure of the vessel (oil
gas separation vessel). This unsafe state shall be preventing, e. g. that can be achieved by the level
transmitter, PLC (BPCS) and control valve (normal operation). In case of not reacting correctly or
failure of this level control loop the dangerous situation of bursting occurred.
There are a couple of solutions, three of them are:

ing correctly or
failure of this level control loop the dangerous situation of bursting occurred.
There are a couple of solutions, three of them are:
• Using a mechanical protection (pressure relief valve), these solution has the disadvantage after
tripping the mechanical device has to be replaced by a new one. Furthermore the employees
around the pressure relief valve can be harmed by the tread out medium.
Implementation of a safety loop (yellow highlighted devices in
Figure 8-3). The safety loop has to quantify (SIL 1/2/3). Instructions are given in IEC 61882 and
IEC 61511-3. After quantifying the safety loop which consists of a set of devices, the design and
validation of the complete loop has to perform according IEC 61508 and IEC 61511 (the relevant
parts).
NOTE All devices should be certified (acc. IEC 61508 - suitable for the intended safety loop).
• Additionally procedures should be placed for the operator or end-user.

Functional safety is achieved when every specified Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is carried out
and the level of performance required of each safety function is met. This is normally achieved by a
process that includes the following steps as a minimum:
1. Identifying what the required SIF are. This means the hazards and safety functions have to be
known. A process of function reviews, formal HAZOP and ‘Accident Reviews’ are applied to
identify these.
2. Assessing the risk-reduction required by the safety function. This will involve a Safety Integrity
Level (SIL) or Performance Level.
3. Ensuring the safety function performs to the design intent, including under conditions of
incorrect operator input and failure modes.

rmance Level.
3. Ensuring the safety function performs to the design intent, including under conditions of
incorrect operator input and failure modes. This will involve having the design and lifecycle
managed by qualified and competent engineers carrying out processes to a recognized
functional safety standard. That standard is IEC 61508, or one of the industry specific
standards derived from IEC 61508 e.g. IEC 61511, or other standard e.g. ISO 13849.
4. Further important documents are:
1. Develop a Safety Requirement Specifications (SRS)
2. Execute Safety Assessment and Validation (V&V plan)
3. Specify procedures for safety Operation and Maintenance (Safety Manual)
Neither safety nor functional safety can be determined without considering the system as a whole and
the environment with which it interacts.
Critical aspects are:
• Sensor / safety PLC / Actor configuration (HFT / SFF, systematic capability)
• Complete execution of safety validation (Installation)
• Execution of period proof tests at all or on calculated time intervals
• Execute well prepared modifications (impact analysis -> risk assessment)

4 Industrial Appliances Application (IAAs)
4.0 General
This chapter identifies the equipment and component selection and installation requirements for new
IAAs and conversion of existing IAAs to natural gas fuel. The standards and codes referenced in this
standard represent the requirements of the American Path.
The following are the key considerations for any gas IAA installation or conversion:
1. Selection of approved or Listed equipment or devices for installation, (including components
used in assemblies).
2. Supply of natural gas at sufficient pressure and volume to the IAA.
3.

ment or devices for installation, (including components
used in assemblies).
2. Supply of natural gas at sufficient pressure and volume to the IAA.
3. Clearance to adjacent materials and structures for maintenance and fire safety.
4. Sufficient air for combustion, ventilation of work chambers and equipment spaces (ventilation
air), and efficient dilution of flue gases and contaminants (dilution air).
5. Venting (removal) of the products of combustion including chimney and flue piping systems.
6. Integration of the IAA and its associated equipment to process, building or equipment controls
and electrical power systems.
7. Conversion of existing fuel burning IAAs to natural gas, including the inspection and cleaning of
chimney and flue piping systems.
8. Operation (normal and emergency conditions), maintenance, and Inspection procedures.
9. Management of change.
4.1 IAA and Equipment Selection
All IAAs shall be designed, constructed and installed to operate safely and present no danger to workers,
the public, processes, or buildings.
4.1.1 High Altitude
Gas input ratings of IAAs shall be used for elevations up to 2000 feet (600 meter). The input ratings of

processes, or buildings.
4.1.1 High Altitude
Gas input ratings of IAAs shall be used for elevations up to 2000 feet (600 meter). The input ratings of
IAAs operating at elevations above 2000 feet (600 meter) shall be reduced in accordance with NFPA 54.
4.1.2 Listed IAAs
IAAs shall be Listed and installed according to the standards in Table 1, or approved by the IB.

4.1.3 Unlisted IAAs
Unlisted IAAs or components shall be approved for use and installation by the IB.
They shall be provided in accordance with functional design requirements identified in referenced
standards and design/installation standards provided in Table 1 and Table 2.
Minimum equipment and installation requirements shall also include the following:
4.1.3.1 Combustion
Initiation of combustion shall not result in flame rollout. Flames shall be stable and completely
within the combustion chamber.
4.1.3.2 Fuel/Air Ratios
Fuel/air ratios shall be in accordance with the recommendations of the manufacturer, and in any
case, the control of the fuel/air ratio shall follow the safety requirements of the relevant standards.

commendations of the manufacturer, and in any
case, the control of the fuel/air ratio shall follow the safety requirements of the relevant standards.
4.1.4 IAA Surface Temperature
Parts of IAA which are intended to be placed in close proximity to the floor or other surfaces shall not
reach temperatures which present danger in the surrounding area.
The surface temperature of knobs and levers of appliances intended to be manipulated shall not present
danger to the user.
Surface temperatures of external parts of IAA intended for industrial use, with the exception of
surfaces or parts which are associated with the transmission of heat, shall not, under operating
conditions, present danger to the user/operator, i.e., the requirements for the appropriate exposure time
as defined in the relevant standards, shall be followed.
4.1.5 Components
4.1.5.1 Safety devices and assemblies that include components for natural gas trains and control
panels shall be Listed or Recognized components.
4.1.5.2 If an IAA contains a natural gas burner with a pilot operated by another fuel, the pilot
shall be considered as part of the IAA system (starting with the manual isolating valve of the pilot

with a pilot operated by another fuel, the pilot
shall be considered as part of the IAA system (starting with the manual isolating valve of the pilot
gas train, up to and including the pilot burner).
4.1.5.3 Fuel trains, controls, and safety components shall be installed such that all devices can be
regularly calibrated and tested efficiently with a minimum of disassembly or specialized
maintenance procedures as per applicable installation requirements.
4.1.5.4 Replacement components of IAAs shall be identical (or at least equivalent) to the
components in the IAA, be listed to the standards in Table 2, or be approved by the IB.

4.1.6 Equivalency
The requirements of this standard are not intended to prohibit the use of any material, method of
construction, or installation procedure not specifically stated by this standard. To demonstrate
equivalency, technical documentation to substantiate that the alternate material, method of
construction, or installation procedure is equivalent or superior in the intended application shall
be submitted to and accepted by the IB. [Source: NFPA 54: 1.4]
4.1.7 Self Certification
Manufacturers of Unlisted equipment shall provide evidence of self-certification with the
applicable design and installation standard as listed in Table 1. Where, e.g., an oven or furnace is
included in the scope of SI 8600, this evidence of self-certification shall include providing the

sted in Table 1. Where, e.g., an oven or furnace is
included in the scope of SI 8600, this evidence of self-certification shall include providing the
completed A4.1(a) and A.4.1(b), Annex A, “Manufacturers Application for Acceptance” form, in
SI 8600, or equivalent form acceptable to the IB.
NOTE The IB has the right to request the opinion of an Expert or a Competent Person,
acceptable to the IB for a review and comments on the self-certification submitted.
4.2 Supply of Natural Gas at Sufficient Pressure and Volume to the IAA
This clause is applicable to new and existing IAAs. (See also chapter 7 for piping).
4.2.1 Pipe Sizing
The gas pressure and volume of the gas supply to the IAA's isolation valve shall be sufficient to
provide the maximum consumption of the IAA at a pressure equal or greater than the minimum
inlet pressure required by the IAA under design operating conditions.
4.2.2 High Supply Pressure
Where the supply pressure exceeds the maximum IAA operating pressure, the pressure shall be
reduced to be at or below the maximum inlet pressure required by the IAA manufacturer, and at
or above the minimum inlet pressure required by the IAA manufacturer.

at or below the maximum inlet pressure required by the IAA manufacturer, and at
or above the minimum inlet pressure required by the IAA manufacturer.
4.2.2.1 Overpressure protection shall be provided in either of the following cases:
(1) When the supply pressure exceeds the pressure rating of any downstream component
(2) When the failure of a single upstream line regulator or service pressure regulator results
in a supply pressure exceeding the pressure rating of any downstream component
4.2.2.2 Overpressure protection shall be provided by any one of the following:

(2) A monitoring regulator installed in combination with a line regulator or service pressure

(3) A full-capacity pressure relief valve,
(4) An overpressure cutoff device, such as a slam-shut valve or a high-pressure switch in
combination with an adequately rated shutoff valve.
4.2.2.3 When a relief valve is used for compliance, the relief valve shall be a full-capacity
relief type.

4.2.2.4 Token relief valves and internal token relief valves shall not be permitted to be used
as the only overpressure protection devices. [Source: NFPA 86: 6.2.7]
NOTE A token relief valve is a pressure relief valve with less than full-capacity relief,
used where pressure increase can occur from regulator leakage or other source.
4.2.3 Low Supply Pressure Protection (compressor suction)
Where the IAA or equipment that is part of the IAA installation can produce a negative pressure in the
gas supply piping, a protective device shall be installed. Such protective devices shall include, but not
be limited to, mechanical, diaphragm-operated, or electrically operated low-pressure shutoff valves.
[Source: NFPA 54: 5.11]
4.2.4 Interconnections for Standby Fuels
Where an IB approved alternate fuel is piped to an IAA, the connection shall be in accordance with
NFPA 54, and shall include means to prevent backflow into unused primary natural gas fuel supply
piping systems that are not in service.

ordance with
NFPA 54, and shall include means to prevent backflow into unused primary natural gas fuel supply
piping systems that are not in service.
4.2.4.1 A three-way valve installed to change to the standby alternate fuel supply and at the same
time shut off the regular supply shall be permitted to be used for this purpose.
[Source: NFPA 54: 5.3.2.2]
4.2.5 IAA Piping Purging Plan
The IB shall be provided with a purge plan for safely removing and or introducing fuel gas that
includes a piping system drawing for the new or converted IAA in accordance with purging
requirements identified in NFPA 54, or NFPA 56. The purge plan shall be approved prior to purging.
In the case of existing plants, purge plans for piping systems shall be created and submitted to the IB
for approval.

4.3 Installation Clearances
Where relevant standards exist (Listed), these standards shall be followed.
Where relevant standards are missing (Un-listed), the IB should receive the demonstration of compliance
by the manufacturer or by the Technical Expert nominated by the End-User, and the IB should give the
approval for compliance.
4.3.1 Clearance to Combustible Materials
4.3.1.1 Listed IAAs shall be installed with clearances in accordance with the manufacturer’s
instructions and with approval from the IB.
4.3.1.2 Unlisted IAAs shall be installed with clearance to combustible materials in accordance
with the manufacturer’s instructions and relevant installation standards. Proposed clearances
shall be submitted to the IB for approval.
4.3.2 Other Clearances
IAAs shall be installed with sufficient clearances with approval from the IB for:
(1) Inspection and maintenance,
(2) Servicing the burner and all parts that require routine inspection,
(3) Combustion air,
(4) Explosion vents, (if required by an installation standard),
(5) Operation of fire protection systems.

ed by an installation standard),
(5) Operation of fire protection systems.
4.4 Sufficient Air for Combustion, Ventilation, and Dilution of Flue Gases
4.4.0 General
This clause is applicable to new and existing IAAs and includes requirements for combustion air,
ventilation of IAAs where necessary, ventilation of the equipment room, and dilution of flue gases if
part of the venting system design.
4.4.1 Natural Draft
Where natural draft IAAs are installed, building openings for air for combustion and ventilation shall
be in accordance with NFPA 54.

4.5 Venting the Products of Combustion
4.5.0 General

4.5.1 New IAA Venting

uctions shall be used.
4.5 Venting the Products of Combustion
4.5.0 General
This clause is applicable to new and existing IAAs.
4.5.1 New IAA Venting
New or converted IAAs shall have venting systems that convey all flue and vent gases (products of
combustion) to the outdoors unless otherwise approved by the IB.
4.5.2 Existing IAA Flue Gas Venting
Where an existing IAA is converted to natural gas from another fuel, the venting system size shall be
evaluated to determine if it is properly sized and designed for the natural gas products of combustion.
The new natural gas fired venting system shall be lined if necessary.
NOTE: When an IAA is converted to natural gas, especially where IAA efficiency is increased,
lower vent temperatures and condensation in the vent or chimney can occur. This can decrease the
useful life of a vent or chimney.
4.5.3 Inspection and Cleaning
Prior to the initial operation of a converted IAA, the vent or chimney serving a converted IAA shall
be inspected to determine that it is clear and unobstructed.
If the vent or chimney is inspected, and a condition report is prepared by a Technical Expert and

ted to determine that it is clear and unobstructed.

submitted to the IB for approval, the IB may conclude that solid material deposits are small enough
not to sufficiently block the vent or chimney or to reduce the flow of flue gases if they all become
dislodged and fall to the bottom of the vent or chimney.

4.6.1 All electrical enclosures, components and cabling shall be suitable for the equipment operating
environment in accordance with NFPA 70.
4.6.2 Power to the electrical ignition, flame safety controls, fuel valves and dampers shall be such that
in the event of power interruption and restoration there will be no unintended release of natural gas
and the IAA will be in a safe condition when power is restored.
4.6.3 IAA wiring, including panel wiring and wiring to individual devices (inputs, outputs, and

4.7 Conversion of Existing Fuel Burning IAAs to Natural Gas
4.7.1 Conversion of existing fuel burning IAAs to natural gas shall either be with a burner Listed to
UL 295, or acceptable to the IB if the burner is less than 400,000 btu input. Converted burners and
IAAs incorporating converted burners shall meet the requirements of 4.7 and all other requirements of
Chapter 4.
4.7.2 Existing IAAs that are converted to natural gas shall undergo a “EOC analysis” to compare the
level of compliance of what is installed to the latest applicable design/installation standard (see
6.4.2.3).
4.7.2.1 The gap analysis deficiencies shall be rank ordered as:
o “A” items, where safety equipment does not exist or is of a type or style that is not compatible
with the most current standard,
o “B” items, where there are deficiencies in procedures, training, or documentation,
o “C” items, where there are deficiencies in testing and inspection,
o “D” items, where there are deficiencies related to marking or identification.

o “C” items, where there are deficiencies in testing and inspection,
o “D” items, where there are deficiencies related to marking or identification.
4.7.2.2 Before the IAA is returned to service, all gaps in “A” deficiencies shall either be brought into
compliance with the latest applicable design/installation standard, or be corrected with equivalent
methods as determined by a risk assessment and approved by the IB before the IAA is returned to
service.

4.7.3 Minimum Requirements for Burners Converted to Natural Gas
4.7.3.1 Burner Selection
Conversion burners shall be selected to provide compatible heat input requirements, turndown,
flame length and shape, and safety requirements of the original burner.
4.7.3.2 Fuel Train Requirements (components compatibility)
Burner fuel train components and safety devices shall be verified to be compatible with natural
gas at the delivered pressure.
4.7.3.3 Combustion Air Requirements
Combustion air provided shall be evaluated to insure that it meets the conversion burner
manufacturer’s requirements and those of the applicable equipment installation standard.
4.7.4 Inspection and Cleaning of Chimneys Serving IAAs Converted to Natural Gas
Prior to initial operation, masonry and metal chimneys and vents serving IAAs that are converted from
another fuel shall be inspected in accordance with NFPA 211, Level 2 requirements and cleaned where
needed as directed by the IB.
4.7.5 Safety Systems Upgrade to Current Standards

d in accordance with NFPA 211, Level 2 requirements and cleaned where
needed as directed by the IB.
4.7.5 Safety Systems Upgrade to Current Standards
The new conversion burners and fuel trains shall be compatible with the existing flame safety system
or burner safety control system, and shall meet the requirements of the current edition of the design or
installation standard.
4.7.6 Re-Commissioning
4.7.6.1 The converted burner, fuel train, and safety systems shall be commissioned to verify that
the converted IAA operates as intended.

1. Verification that fuel/air ratios meet the manufacturers’ specifications requirements at all

- for modulated burners: 10% increments from low fire to high fire and back;
- for non-modulated burners: low fire and high fire (according to the firing level design)
2. Documentation of the flue gas composition at each firing rate and its compliance with all
emissions requirements,

ng to the firing level design)
2. Documentation of the flue gas composition at each firing rate and its compliance with all

3. Documentation of all combustion and purge air flow, pressures, timing and purge air flow

Flue gas testing, documentation of fuel/air ratios, shall be conducted according to a protocol
approved in advance by the IB. This protocol shall include consideration of the equipment
operating conditions (i.e. warm-up), verification of adequate combustion air, how long each
firing increment is to be held before testing, location of test ports in the stack, and verification
that the appropriate instruments are in use and are properly calibrated.
4.7.6.3 Derivation and documentation of set points for all safety devices shall include the high
and low gas pressure safety and combustion air proving devices.
4.7.6.4 Positioning of the flame detection device and ignitor shall be to allow ignition of pilot
and main flames in accordance with the applicable installation standards.
4.7.6.5 Verification of flame detectors' selection and installation shall be according to

s in accordance with the applicable installation standards.
4.7.6.5 Verification of flame detectors' selection and installation shall be according to
manufacturers’ installation instructions. This consideration shall include, at a minimum, the
need for self-checking detectors, maximum ambient temperatures for the devices, and proper
flame sighting. If ultraviolet optical detector technology is in use, a pilot spark pickup test shall
verify that the detector is sighted properly with no spark pickup during trial for ignition.
4.7.6.6 Seat leakage tests shall be completed on all manual and automatic valves within the fuel
train system before turning equipment over for operation, and it shall be verified that no valves
leak through when in the closed position at more than the valve manufacturers Listing standard
requirements.
4.7.6.7 A complete test of all of the fuel train and IAA safety systems shall be conducted.
4.7.6.8 A leak check of all fuel piping associated with the IAA, from the equipment isolation
valve to the burner, shall be conducted at operating pressures.

4.8 Operation, Maintenance and Inspection
4.8.1 Operation – Initial Start-up
4.8.1.1 Burner management system safety logic shall be tested and verified to be in compliance
with the design.
4.8.1.2 The initial startup of all IAAs shall be in accordance with the IAA manufacturers’
recommendations and all startup requirements in the IAA standard.
4.8.1.3 Prior to startup all natural gas piping incorporated in the IAA shall be inspected for
leakage.
4.8.1.4 The set points of all safety interlocks shall be documented and verified to be in accordance
with the commissioning requirements prior to initial startup.
4.8.1.5 If it is necessary to adjust the burner input or combustion air flow of an IAA, the
procedures in the IAA installation standard or manufacturers installation documents shall be
followed. Where such procedures are not available, the procedures in NFPA 54 for adjusting the
burner input shall be followed.
4.8.2 Operation – Ongoing
4.8.2.1 All IAAs shall be operated in accordance with the design parameters and applicable
standards.

ut shall be followed.
4.8.2 Operation – Ongoing
4.8.2.1 All IAAs shall be operated in accordance with the design parameters and applicable
standards.
4.8.2.2 Operating instructions and documentation shall be available to operators and shall
include:
(1) Schematic piping and wiring diagrams,
(2) Startup procedures,
(3) Shutdown procedures,
(4) Emergency procedures, including those occasioned by loss of special atmospheres, electric
power, inert gas, or other essential utilities,
(5) Normal and emergency operating instructions,
(6) Maintenance procedures, including interlock and valve seat leakage tightness testing
procedures,
(7) Equipment commissioning data including desired flue gas compositions, safety device set
points, and purge timing.
4.8.2.3 Where the IAA is designed to be operated in different modes, operating procedures shall
be provided for each operating mode, for changes to different operating modes, and for a change
to alternate fuels.

4.8.3 Operation - Training
4.8.3.1 All IAA operators shall be trained annually in the operation of the IAA, Including
start-up, shutdown, and lockout procedures. Training shall be documented and shall include
evidence of knowledge transfer, (e.g. test or exam). The owner or other responsible party
shall retain training records for a minimum of 3 years.
4.8.3.2 All employees who operate, maintain, or supervise the operation and maintenance of
the IAA shall demonstrate an understanding of the IAA, it’s operation, and safe operating
procedures applicable to their job functions.
4.8.3.3 All IAA operators shall receive refresher training prior to startup after any changes

procedures applicable to their job functions.
4.8.3.3 All IAA operators shall receive refresher training prior to startup after any changes
that affect the operational safety of the IAA, (see 4.9).
4.8.4 Inspection, Testing, and Maintenance
4.8.4.1 Safety devices shall be maintained in accordance with the IAA manufacturer’s
instructions and all applicable installation standards.
4.8.4.2 All safety interlocks shall be tested for function at least annually.
4.8.4.3 All gas valves, including valve proving systems, shall be seat leakage tested at least
annually.
4.8.4.4 The IAA maintenance program shall include the frequency and extent of inspection,
testing, and repair procedures and shall meet the minimum requirements of the applicable
installation standard.
4.8.4.5 Safety device testing shall be documented at least annually with trip designed versus
actual trip points and rates of gas valve seat leakage after valve seat tightness testing
documented to demonstrate compliance.
4.8.4.6 The set point of temperature, pressure, or flow devices used as safety interlocks shall
be verified to be in accordance with documented commissioning requirements at least
annually.

4.9 Management of Change Procedure
A management of change procedure shall be prepared. This procedure shall address the impact of newly
installed IAAs, the conversion of IAAs, and/or changes to existing IAAs, components, operating and
maintenance procedures and anything else that could impact gas piping or equipment safety.

and/or changes to existing IAAs, components, operating and
maintenance procedures and anything else that could impact gas piping or equipment safety.
This procedure shall specify, among others, the instructions to guide the user with respect to the activities
needed during a performance of a change in the plant, including:
• Mapping all the means (expected and unexpected) that are due to be affected by the change,
• Assessing all the impacts relevant to performing the change,
• Defining "stopping points" to examine and to define whether the change is needed and how to
promote it (including the relevant complementary steps), before performing it,
• Validating the relevant procedures to the activity.

6. Testing and Approval
6.1 General
This chapter outlines the process of Testing and Approval of IAAs.
Listed IAAs may be installed with no further testing.

ting and Approval
6.1 General
This chapter outlines the process of Testing and Approval of IAAs.
Listed IAAs may be installed with no further testing. The goal of this chapter is to provide the regulatory
and technical requirements and processes to approve natural gas IAA selections that are not Listed and the
installation of Listed and Unlisted IAAs in industrial premises for safe use.
NOTE The goal of this chapter is to make sure that the regulatory and technical requirements and
processes to approve natural gas IAAs in industrial premises for safe use are clear.

Standard SI 6464 is applicable to the American path of this Israeli Standard.
