Introduction
This checklist provides an organized method to assist with confirming that the requirements of NFPA 37 (2015) are
addressed and complied with in reviewing the installation of natural gas burning stationary engines or gas turbines in
Israel. It is not an exact transcription of all of the issues contained in that document. This checklist document is an
abbreviated and condensed representation of NFPA 37’s requirements. This checklist is not intended to replace NFPA

ent. This checklist document is an
abbreviated and condensed representation of NFPA 37’s requirements. This checklist is not intended to replace NFPA
37, and if conflicts between this checklist and NFPA 37 are identified, the specific requirements in NFPA 37 are to be
used. It will be necessary for the user to refer to the actual NFPA 37 document to verify that all issues have been
addressed.

Introduction
This checklist provides an organized method to assist with confirming that the requirements of NFPA 85 are addressed
and complied with in reviewing the installation of boilers with a heat input of over 12,500,000 BTUH (3.66 MW) in Israel.
It is not an exact transcription of the entire document; instead it is an abbreviated and condensed representation of the
requirements relevant to natural gas for a fuel and some dual fuel applications. This checklist includes requirements for

ensed representation of the
requirements relevant to natural gas for a fuel and some dual fuel applications. This checklist includes requirements for
other fuels that may be relevant where alternate fuels are in use. This checklist is not intended to replace NFPA 85, and
if conflicts between this checklist and NFPA 85 are identified, use the specific requirements in NFPA 85.
Use of this checklist:
 Complete the questions in chapter 4 for all types of equipment.
 Complete the questions in chapter 5 where a single burner boiler is being installed
 Complete the questions in chapter 6 were a boiler with more than one burner is being installed
 Complete the question in Chapter 8 where a Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG) is being installed. Chapter
8 applies to all fired and unfired heat recovery steam generator (HRSG) systems or other combustion turbine
exhaust systems and their associated combustion turbines.
Chapters 1 through 4 and 8 do not dictate the methods or details of the combustion turbine manufacturer's product or
control system.
Chapter 8 does not apply directly to fired boilers but instead covers heat recovery steam generators used to recover

rer's product or
control system.
Chapter 8 does not apply directly to fired boilers but instead covers heat recovery steam generators used to recover
heat from processes flue gases from ovens and furnaces or most commonly combustion turbines. The chapter does not
address multiple combustion turbines exhausting into a single HRSG, nor does it address single or multiple combustion
turbines exhausting into multiple HRSGs through a header. Any system applying this concept requires special design
considerations that are not addressed in chapter 8. Chapter 8 identifies specific functional considerations for proper
interfacing related to the safety aspects of the combined combustion turbine and the HRSG or other combustion turbine
exhaust systems.

NOTE: A Class 1 igniter is intended to ignite the burner under all light-off or operating conditions
(usually with 10% or more of the burner input).
A Class 2 igniter is normally used to ignite the burner under prescribed conditions (usually 4 – 10%
of burner input), and to support combustion under low loads and adverse operating conditions.
A Class 3 igniter is a small igniter used for gas and fuel oil burners to ignite under specific light-off
conditions.
NOTE: Class 1 igniters can be used as Class 2 or Class 3 igniters. Class 2 igniters can
be used as Class 3 igniters.

Chapter 6 - Multiple Burner Boilers
Application:
 This chapter is used in conjunction with Chapter 4, Fundamentals, and requires the coordination of operating
procedures, control systems, interlocks, and structural design. Where conflicts exist, the requirements of this

equires the coordination of operating
procedures, control systems, interlocks, and structural design. Where conflicts exist, the requirements of this
chapter apply.
 The sections of this code that are common to all fuels to be burned in a multiple burner boiler are used in
conjunction with those sections that are applicable to a specific fuel being utilized.
 Section 6.5 applies to minimizing the risk of negative furnace draft in excess of furnace structural capability.
 Section 6.6 applies to burner management, combustion control systems, and operating procedures for multiple
burner boilers utilizing fuel gas as defined in 3.3.64.
 Section 6.7 applies to burner management, combustion control systems, and operating procedures for multiple
burner boilers utilizing fuel oil as defined in 3.3.63.3.
 Section 6.8 applies to burner management, combustion control systems. and operating procedures for multiple
burner boilers utilizing pulverized coal fuel as defined in 3.3.63.2.1.
 Chapter 6 also applies to fired flue gas re-heaters (duct burners) installed in the flue gas path downstream of a
multiple burner boiler furnace.
Purpose:

er 6 also applies to fired flue gas re-heaters (duct burners) installed in the flue gas path downstream of a
multiple burner boiler furnace.
Purpose:
 The purpose of chapter 6 is to contribute to operating safety and to prevent furnace explosions and implosions
in multiple burner boiler installations from the forced draft fan inlet(s) to the stack inlet.
 Chapter 6 establishes minimum requirements to be followed for the design, installation, operation, and
maintenance of multiple burner boilers and their fuel-burning, air supply and combustion products removal
systems.
 The user of this chapter must recognize the complexity of firing fuel with regard to the type of equipment and
the characteristics of the fuel.
 General requirements for mechanical equipment are to be in accordance with Sections 4.6 through 4.16.
 Forced draft (FD) and induced draft (ID) fans referenced in this chapter include all fans whose purpose is to
supply air for combustion or remove products of combustion, including associated booster fans, and excludes
fans in the pulverized coal system.

Chapter 6, Section 6.6
General: The requirements of Section 6.6 apply to installations where fuel gas is supplied.
Fuel Gas Firing, Special Problems:
 Fuel gas is colorless and some fuel gas may be odorless; therefore, a leak is not usually visually detectable and in
addition, detection of a fuel gas leak by means of odor is unreliable.
- Within buildings creates hazardous conditions, particularly where the fuel gas piping is routed through confined
areas.
- Outdoor boilers tend to minimize confined-area problems.

rdous conditions, particularly where the fuel gas piping is routed through confined
areas.
- Outdoor boilers tend to minimize confined-area problems.
 The nature of fuel gas makes it possible to experience severe departures from design air-fuel ratios without any
visible evidence at the burners, furnace, or stack that could escalate into a progressively worsening (and dangerous)
condition.
- Combustion control systems that respond to reduced boiler steam pressure or steam flow with an impulse for
more fuel, unless protected or interlocked to prevent a fuel-rich mixture, are potentially hazardous.
- Special requirements, as defined in NFPA 54, must be taken with wet fuel gas systems.
NOTE: Commercially available utility supplied natural gas is not usually wet. (If in doubt, contact the gas
supplier
 Widely differing characteristics of fuel gas from either a single source or multiple sources could result in significant
change in the heat input rate to the burners without a corresponding change in airflow.
 Relief valves and atmospheric vents must discharge to atmosphere in accordance with 4.9.1.

o the burners without a corresponding change in airflow.
 Relief valves and atmospheric vents must discharge to atmosphere in accordance with 4.9.1.
 Fuel gas piping must be purged prior to and after maintenance and repair, as detailed in NFPA 54 and NFPA 56.
Purging into and out of service must be done according to NFPA 56 for fuel gas systems operating at more than 125
psig and NFPA 54 for systems operating at less than 125 psig.

Chapter 8, Heat Recovery Steam Generators and Other Combustion Turbine Exhaust Systems
The purpose of chapter 8 is:
 To contribute to operation safety.
 To prevent explosions, implosions, and uncontrolled fires in HRSG sections and other exhaust system ductwork.
 To establish minimum requirements for the design, installation, operation and maintenance of heat recovery
steam generators and their fuel-burning, air supply, and combustion products removal systems
 To require the coordination of operating procedures and components, control systems, interlocks, and
structural design

ion products removal systems
 To require the coordination of operating procedures and components, control systems, interlocks, and
structural design
The user of NFPA 85 must recognize the complexity of firing with regard to the type of equipment used and the
characteristics of the fuel. Emphasis must be placed on the importance of component coordination and on knowledge of
expected operating conditions.

NOTE: Section 8.8 applies where gaseous or liquid fuels are being burned in HRSG or other combustion turbine exhaust
systems, and these requirements include interlocks for ensuring prescribed action, burner management system trips,
flame detection, and an indication of the status of the operating sequences. In addition, the requirements of Section 8.8
apply to the design, installation, and operation of duct burners in HRSG systems. No specific degree of automation
beyond the minimum specified safeguards is defined or required, because this is subject to factors such as, but not
limited to, physical size of the unit, use of the central control room, degree of reliability required, and experience level
of operating personnel.

52.1.6.1 (1) Due to the physical size, shape, and mass, a damper cannot be
assumed to fully seal a combustion turbine exhaust gas flow path.
52.1.6.2 (2) A leaking fuel valve or a combustion turbine false start will result in
an explosive mixture in the HRSG on either or both sides of a damper
during shutdown periods.
52.1.6.3 (3) Failure to purge an explosive mixture prior to introduction of hot
turbine exhaust gas will result in ignition of the mixture. Even at full
speed and no load, the combustion turbine exhaust temperatures can

on of hot
turbine exhaust gas will result in ignition of the mixture. Even at full
speed and no load, the combustion turbine exhaust temperatures can
be above the autoignition temperatures for combustion turbine and
HRSG fuels.
52.1.6.4 (4) Systems using two stacks (HRSG and bypass), with or without
dampers, tend to induce a reverse flow of fresh air through the HRSG as
a result of the high temperature combustion turbine exhaust gas flow
up the bypass stack.
52.1.6.5 (5) Failure of damper-operating mechanisms will allow instantaneous
reversal of damper position (from open position to closed position and
vice versa) due to aerodynamic effects with the following results:
52.1.6.5 (5a) Sudden closure of an operating combustion turbine free exhaust
path will result in high transient duct internal pressures.
52.1.6.6 (5b) These high pressures can cause distortion of the HRSG enclosure
and rupture of the duct expansion joints.

SI 6464 (2017)

Introduction
This checklist provides an organized method to assist with confirming that the requirements of
NFPA 86 (2015) are addressed and complied with in reviewing the installation of an oven or
furnace in Israel. It is not an exact transcription of all of the issues contained in this document.
This checklist document is an abbreviated and condensed representation of the requirements
relevant to natural gas oven, furnace, and thermal oxidizer installations. This checklist is not
intended to replace NFPA 86, and if conflicts between this checklist and NFPA 86 are identified,
the specific requirements in NFPA 86 are to be used. It will be necessary for the user to refer to
the actual NFPA 86 document to verify that all issues have been addressed.

s in NFPA 86 are to be used. It will be necessary for the user to refer to
the actual NFPA 86 document to verify that all issues have been addressed.
When applying this checklist users should complete the questions from chapters 4 through 9
for all types of equipment. In addition, chapters 10 thru 14 should be used for the specific types
of equipment indicated for those chapters. This checklist does include issues related to other
fuels that may be relevant where alternate fuels are in use.טבעי

424
Introduction
This checklist provides an organized method to assist with confirming that an IAA’s venting system complies
with the NFPA 54 and NFPA 211 system design and installation requirements are addressed. This checklist
document is an abbreviated and condensed representation of the requirements relevant to chimneys and
vents;(it is not an exact transcription of the NFPA 54 and NFPA 211 requirements). NFPA 54 and NFPA 211
contain many sections that are relevant to residential chimneys and vents because these present a significant
possibility of fire and the health concerns from excessive carbon monoxide in buildings. Sections of these
documents that referred to residential equipment and installation issues are not included as this checklist
document was focuses on industrial and large commercial appliances.
This checklist is not intended to replace venting and chimney requirements in other relevant and related

industrial and large commercial appliances.
This checklist is not intended to replace venting and chimney requirements in other relevant and related
documents like NFPA 86 and NFPA 85.If conflicts between this checklist and these or other documents are
identified, the equipment specific documents requirements are to be used. In addition to completing this

Glossary of venting terms.
Equivalent Length chimney / vent sizing method ‐ appliance manufacturer specifies max number of
"equivalent feet" of vent permitted to be attached to the appliance, then the proposed vent / chimney
system is checked for total equivalent length based upon summation of total lengths of straight sections

appliance, then the proposed vent / chimney
system is checked for total equivalent length based upon summation of total lengths of straight sections
plus additional "equivalent" lengths for various other parts like elbows, tees, termination caps, etc. If
"equivalent" length of system is less than manufacturer specifies as maximum, system is acceptable. If
proposed layout creates more than max permitted "equivalent length" system is not permitted and a larger
diameter, alternate layout, mechanical exhauster or other engineered system approach to vent sizing is
required in order to assure safe venting.
For
Information
only
ASHRAE Chimney / Vent sizing method ‐ most accurate by far but requires numerous data concerning
appliance operating conditions. Accounts for natural draft created within system as well as maximum
backpressure and draft requirements at appliance outlet, then factors in flow resistance associated with the
various system parts and dimensions (for the rate at which products of combustion are being produced).
Requires accurate knowledge of: CO2, Flue Gas Temperature, Draft requirements at appliance outlet,

which products of combustion are being produced).
Requires accurate knowledge of: CO2, Flue Gas Temperature, Draft requirements at appliance outlet,
system layout including all fittings (elbows, tees, termination, etc.) and dimensions.

ISRAELI STANDARD SI 6464

January 2017

Amendment No. 1

April 2017

Industrial natural gas consuming appliances - Safety Requirements for appliances application, workplace environment, testing and approval, and natural gas supply pipework

Amendment revision notice

This Amendment updates Israeli standard SI 6464 of January 2017

Updating the Standard

Israeli standards are reviewed periodically at least every five years, in order to adapt them to scientific and technological developments. Users of Standards should ascertain that they are in possession of the latest edition of the Standard including its Amendments. 

A document appearing in the "Reshumot" (The Israeli Official Journal) as an Amendment may be a separate Amendment, or an Amendment incorporated into the Standard.

Standard validity

An Israeli standard including revisions takes effect from its publication date in "Reshumot".

Whether the document or parts of it are Official should be checked. An Official Standard or Amendment (in whole or in part) takes effect 60 days following publication of the notice in the "Reshumot", unless the notice states otherwise for the effective date.

Standards mark

kes effect 60 days following publication of the notice in the "Reshumot", unless the notice states otherwise for the effective date.

Standards mark

A manufacturer of a product complying with the requirements of the applicable Israeli standards is entitled, after being licensed by the Standards Institution of Israel, to mark it with the Standards mark:

Copyright

This Standard or any part of it may not be photocopied, copied or published by any means whatsoever, without prior permission in writing of the Standards Institution of Israel. ©

NOTE: This Amendment only applies to the European path of the Israeli Standard SI 6464.The parallel Amendment to the American path is to be completed.

SECTION 1 - EUROPEAN PATH

2. Normative References

The following shall be added to the list of European standards:

EN 50156-1

-

The following shall be added to the list of National Standards and Documents:

IGE/ UP/ 9

-

Application of Natural Gas and fuel oil systems to gas turbines and supplementary and auxiliary fired burners

3. Terms & Definitions

IGE/ UP/ 9

-

Application of Natural Gas and fuel oil systems to gas turbines and supplementary and auxiliary fired burners

3. Terms & Definitions

3.36 "Gassed Up" Plants

plants pertaining IAAs, which already consume natural gas, that were not tested by the Israeli Standards Institution, but their piping was granted with the approval of the Gas Authority in the Ministry of National Infrastructures, Energy and Water Resources.

The definition shall be deleted and replaced by the following:

3.36 "Gassed-Up" IAAs

IAAs which have been consuming natural gas as of prior to December 1, 2015, or; IAAs which have not initiated the consumption of natural gas as of prior to such date, however have been granted prior to such date an approval for consumption of natural gas by the Gas Authority in the Ministry of National Infrastructures, Energy and Water Resources and according to the Gas Law (Safety and Licensing) 1989.

6.4.5. Safety Test for the Renewal of the "Serviceability Approval" in IAAs Already Consuming Natural Gas ("Gassed Up IAAs”)

The following shall be added:

6.4.5.1 General

he Renewal of the "Serviceability Approval" in IAAs Already Consuming Natural Gas ("Gassed Up IAAs”)

The following shall be added:

6.4.5.1 General

In accordance with the Gas Law (Safety and Licensing) 1989, Gassed-Up IAAs (see 3.36) shall undergo the tests specified in Annex A, considering previous approvals granted to such IAAs. The IB shall assure completion of the tests in Annex A, and upon approval, the IB shall issue to the End-User the "Serviceability Approval" (see 3.35).

See an example of a "Serviceability Approval" in Annex B.

6.4.5.2 Gas Turbines

Gas turbines shall be tested according to IGE/UP/9 and according to the additional following standards: SI 74600 part 2, IEC 61508 (the relevant part), EN 50156 part 1.

Pressure equipment shall comply with the requirements in 4.2.2.

Pressure equipment containing natural gas, shall comply with the requirements in 4.2.2.

The IB will exercise his expertise and experience in conjunction with the above guidelines, in order to demonstrate "fit to purpose" of the natural gas system of the GT, and issue a SERVICEABILITY APPROVAL issued for NATURAL GAS APPLIANCE.

entilation as given in chapter 5 of this standard,

are "fit to purpose".

Amendment No. 1 to SI 6464 (2018)
2
In accordance with the Gas Law (Safety and Licensing) 1989, Gassed -Up IAAs (see 3.36)
shall undergo a test and evaluation in order to demonstrate their safety, considering existing
approvals, if such are available.
The IB shall develop a testing and evaluation checklist for each IAA, based on the
applicable standard, as per the Normative References standards list in this Is raeli Standard,
SI 6464, or other relevant standards / technical documents used in each IAA.
When a standard / technical document not included in the Normative References standards
list of this Israeli Standard is used, the IB shall provide detailed justifications for using this
standard / technical document, including the means to assure the IAA's safety using this
standard / technical document.
An example for testing based on the Israeli Standard SI 74600-2 is given in Annex 6.1.
For the testing and evaluation of gas turbines, Annex 6.2 may be used.
The IB shall assure the successful completion of the tests, and issue the End-User with a
"Serviceability Approval" upon approval (see 3.35).
A "Serviceability Approval" form is given in Annex 6.3.

Amendment revision notice

This Amendment updates Israeli standard SI 6464 of January 2017

Updating the Standard

Israeli standards are reviewed periodically at least every five years, in order to adapt them to scientific and technological developments. Users of Standards should ascertain that they are in possession of the latest edition of the Standard including its Amendments. 

A document appearing in the "Reshumot" (The Israeli Official Journal) as an Amendment may be a separate Amendment, or an Amendment incorporated into the Standard.

Standard validity

umot" (The Israeli Official Journal) as an Amendment may be a separate Amendment, or an Amendment incorporated into the Standard.

Standard validity

An Israeli standard including revisions takes effect from its publication date in "Reshumot".

Whether the document or parts of it are Official should be checked. An Official Standard or Amendment (in whole or in part) takes effect 60 days following publication of the notice in the "Reshumot", unless the notice states otherwise for the effective date.

Standards mark

A manufacturer of a product complying with the requirements of the applicable Israeli standards is entitled, after being licensed by the Standards Institution of Israel, to mark it with the Standards mark:

Copyright

This Standard or any part of it may not be photocopied, copied or published by any means whatsoever, without prior permission in writing of the Standards Institution of Israel. ©

3. Terms & Definitions

3.36 "Gassed Up" Plants

The definition shall be deleted and replaced by the following:

3.36 "Gassed-Up" IAAs

IAAs which have been consuming natural gas as of prior to December 1, 2015, or;

IAAs which have not initiated the consumption of natural gas as of prior to this date, however have been granted prior to this date with an approval for consumption of natural gas by the Natural Gas Authority in the Ministry of Energy and according to the Gas Law (Safety and Licensing) 1989.

6.4.5. Safety Test for the Renewal of the "Serviceability Approval" in IAAs Already Consuming Natural Gas ("Gassed Up IAAs”)

The title of clause 6.4.5 shall be deleted and replaced by the following:

bility Approval" in IAAs Already Consuming Natural Gas ("Gassed Up IAAs”)

The title of clause 6.4.5 shall be deleted and replaced by the following:

6.4.5 Safety Test for the "Serviceability Approval" in IAAs Already Consuming Natural Gas ("Gassed Up IAAs”)

The following shall be added:

6.4.5.1 IAAs

In accordance with the Gas Law (Safety and Licensing) 1989, Gassed-Up IAAs (see 3.36) shall undergo a test and evaluation in order to demonstrate their safety, considering existing approvals, if such are available.

The IB shall develop a testing and evaluation checklist for each IAA, based on applicable standards. 6.0

Supply of pre-mixed fuel gas / air

6.1

Mixture pipework

The mixture pipework volume shall be as small as possible. The system shall be designed so as to provide a sufficiently high mixture flow velocity such that flame propagation upstream cannot occur, or shall be fitted with a flame trap/arrestor or pressure relief devices.

Alternatively, the system shall be fitted with a sensor which causes lock-out in the event of the flow velocity falling below a pre-determined limit or a temperature sensor which causes lock-out in the event of flash-back or spit-back. These devices are not required for burners where the manufacturer can demonstrate that flash-back cannot occur in any circumstances (e.g. pilot burners with their own mixing devices).

6.2

Air and gas supply of the mixture circuit

hat flash-back cannot occur in any circumstances (e.g. pilot burners with their own mixing devices).

6.2

Air and gas supply of the mixture circuit

The presence of fuel gas/air mixture in the pipework supplying either fuel gas or air to the mixing device, whether due to reverse flow of the mixture, ingress to either feed or to internal leakage, shall be prevented.

If a non-return valve is used for this purpose and if it is not resistant to flashback, then an additional high gas pressure switch located downstream of this non-return valve shall be incorporated to shut off the flow of fuel

gas to the equipment by means of the relevant automatic shut-off valves.

A flash-back shall trigger an alarm. The required measures after a flash-back shall be described in the instruction for use.

לכב'

מר יעקב ווכטל – הממונה על התקינה

משרד הכלכלה

הנדון: תקינת דוודי קיטור לעומת תקינתם לגבי גז טבעי

א.נ.

אנו מהווים גוף בודק למערכות גז טבעי לפי ת"י 6464.

דוודי קיטור חדשים או משומשים (מסוג צינורות אש) אשר עוברים הסבה מדלק כלשהו לגז טבעי ואשר משתמשים במבערים מאולצים, ההסבה או קניית המערכת החדשה נעשית לפי תקן EN 676 על נספחיו (שאליו מפנה ת"י 6464) כולל נספח K. לפי נספח זה יש לוודא שקיימות הגנות בטיחותיות (כמו שפל מים ועודף לחץ) על המתקן כאשר רמת האמינות הנדרשת היא אקוויוולנטיות ל SIL 3.

לעומת זאת, קיימים תקנים ישראליים אחרים לדוודים אלו כמו ת"י 12953-7 וגם ת"י 4280 אשר בהם אין כל התייחסות לרמת אמינות זו.

יש לציין שלדוודי קיטור אלה יש הגנות (חיגורים) בין הפונקציות הבטיחותיות האלה לפעולת המבער והן נבדקות תקופתית ע"י בודק דוודים מוסמך, כולל בדיקת מכון הקיטור, אך לאו דווקא לרמת SIL 3.

מכיוון שכך, אנו רואים בתקנים הישראליים הרלבנטיים המתייחסים ישירות לדוודי קיטור אלו ושלפיהם הם נקנים ומאושרים ע"י מת"י ונבדקים ומאושרים ע"י בודק מוסמך כגוברים על כל תקן אחר ואנו נאלצים לוותר על דרישת ת"י 6464 לרמת SIL 3 ומקבלים רמת אמינות נמוכה יותר.

אנו מבקשים לסנכרן בין התקנים כך שניתן יהיה להסתמך על ת"י 6464 במלואו.
